My honest advice please don't waste your money on Tata Evs instead buy some other company vehicle.

Instant torque response in city traffic is excellent and makes driving enjoyable.
Rear seat knees-up posture is a bad ergonomic issue that passengers consistently complain about.
433-litre boot combined with 22-litre frunk is very good practical storage for a family EV.
No wireless Android Auto despite wireless charging pad is a very bad and confusing omission.
8-year battery warranty gives very good long-term ownership confidence and peace of mind.
Suspension firmness over potholes is worse than the ICE Creta and feels like a bad regression.
Ventilated front seats are excellent and genuinely impactful during Indian summers.
ADAS false alerts in slow city traffic are frequent and bad enough that most owners disable the system.
Cabin features are very good overall but the 50kW DC charging speed is bad for highway trips.
Range on 51.4kWh is excellent in city but real highway range with AC is worse than ARAI figures.
Service network is very good but rear seat comfort is bad for taller adult passengers.
Hyundai build quality is very good but suspension tuning over rough roads is noticeably worse.
Bose audio and dual screens are excellent but no wireless Android Auto is a bad daily irritant.

50kW DC charging is the worst aspect of the Creta Electric. Waiting 60 minutes at a highway charger for 10 to 80 percent is a bad experience. Every competitor with 100kW charging makes road trips far more practical. This is very bad for a premium segment car in 2024.

The Creta Electric Excellence is a very good family EV. Panoramic sunroof, ventilated seats, ADAS, excellent boot and 51.4kWh range in one car is impressive. Hyundai service adds excellent peace of mind. Running costs are very good and savings versus petrol are real.

Rear seat ergonomics are genuinely bad. The floor is raised because of the battery and adult rear passengers sit with their knees uncomfortably elevated. On journeys beyond 90 minutes rear passengers consistently report bad leg fatigue. For a family SUV this is a very bad flaw.

The Creta Electric drives very well in city traffic. Silent cabin and instant torque make commuting excellent. BlueLink remote pre-cooling in Chennai summers is a very good feature that works reliably. Battery efficiency in city conditions is excellent and running costs are impressively low.

Wired-only Android Auto in 2024 is a very bad decision by Hyundai. The car has wireless charging on the console pad but makes you plug in a USB cable every single time for projection. This is a bad contradiction that frustrates buyers who paid premium for a feature-rich product.

The 51.4kWh battery performance is excellent and delivers very good range across all conditions. City efficiency of around 6km per kWh is impressive. Home charging overnight is very convenient. The Creta Electric is a very good choice for daily city commuters.

Suspension tuning is bad compared to the ICE Creta. Owners who previously drove the petrol version are shocked at the worse ride quality over bad roads. Potholes transmit a harsh thud through the cabin that the petrol Creta never did. This is a very bad regression for ride comfort.

Level 2 ADAS on the Creta Electric is very good. Adaptive cruise with stop-and-go works excellently on expressways. The blind spot camera feed on the digital cluster is an excellent safety innovation. ADAS has genuinely improved my driving safety and highway driving is much less tiring.

Real world ARAI claimed 473km versus actual 300km at highway speed with AC is a bad gap. This misleads buyers expecting genuine long-range capability. The 42kWh variant's real highway range being only 280km is very bad for a car marketed as an all-round family EV.

Hyundai service experience is very good and among the best in the Indian EV industry. Staff are EV trained and the BlueLink diagnostic system makes service visits efficient. 8-year battery warranty is excellent. Service support adds very good confidence to the ownership proposition.
Charging speed is bad.
Range in city is very good.
Rear seat is uncomfortable.
Cabin features are excellent.
No wireless Android Auto is bad.
Service network is very good.
Suspension over potholes is worse than ICE version.
ADAS features work well on highways.
Running cost savings are very good.
Overall acceptable EV for city use.

After 14 months in Delhi NCR the Creta Electric has been excellent for daily commuting. The 51.4kWh battery consistently delivers very good range above 400km in city conditions. BlueLink connected features are very good and reliable. Running cost savings versus my earlier petrol SUV are impressive and exceed Rs 1.2 lakh. Overall a very good EV ownership experience.

The 50kW DC fast charging is genuinely bad for highway travel. I drove from Pune to Goa and the charging stops added nearly two hours to the journey. At each stop the 60-minute wait was a bad experience. Hyundai should be embarrassed that a premium car at this price has worse charging speed than many competitors at lower price points.

The Creta Electric Excellence is a very good product overall. Panoramic sunroof quality is excellent. Ventilated seats in Rajasthan summers are very good. The 433-litre boot handles our family luggage excellently. BlueLink remote pre-cooling before getting in is very good and reliable. Highly recommend for family buyers.

Rear seat ergonomics are very bad on the Creta Electric. My family of four struggled on a three-hour drive to Jaipur because rear passengers had a bad knees-up posture throughout. Adult passengers said the rear seat experience was worse than budget hatchbacks. This is an unacceptable design issue at this price.

Hyundai's service network is very good and was the main reason I chose Creta Electric over rivals. Service centre in my city has EV-trained technicians and every scheduled visit has been handled excellently. Battery health at 12 months shows very good retention. Very happy with the after-sales support.

No wireless Android Auto is a very bad oversight. The car has a wireless charging pad for phones but forces a wired USB connection for Android Auto projection. This is bad design logic that frustrates me every single day. A software OTA update should fix this but Hyundai has been bad at responding to this feedback.

The Creta Electric is excellent for city driving in Bengaluru. Instant torque in traffic is very good and the silent cabin transforms the commuting experience. Efficiency at around 5.8km per kWh in city mode is excellent. Home charging overnight means the car is always ready. Very good daily EV.

Suspension firmness is worse than I expected based on the ICE Creta reputation for comfort. Sharp potholes and bad road sections send jarring impacts into the cabin. The suspension tuning feels like a very bad compromise made to accommodate the battery weight. Ride quality on Indian roads is noticeably worse than the petrol version.

The Bose 8-speaker audio system is excellent and the dual 10.25-inch screens are very good in terms of clarity and responsiveness. Feature density in the Excellence variant is impressive and gives very good value. The cabin overall feels very premium for the price. Very happy with the feature package.

ADAS false braking alerts in city traffic are very bad and startling. The forward collision system triggers unnecessarily in bumper-to-bumper traffic which is a bad and potentially dangerous experience. Most Creta Electric owners I know have disabled the ADAS in city mode because the false alerts are worse than the benefit.
51.4kWh battery range is excellent and removes city range anxiety completely.
50kW DC charging makes highway trips unnecessarily long and is bad for long-distance travel.
Panoramic sunroof quality and size is very good and transforms cabin ambience.
Rear seat under-thigh support is bad and causes discomfort on long journeys.
BlueLink remote pre-cooling feature works very well and is excellent in Indian summers.
Wired-only Android Auto is a bad daily annoyance that should not exist at this price point.
Hyundai service reliability is very good and gives excellent EV ownership confidence.
Suspension on rough roads is worse than petrol Creta and sends bad thuds through cabin.
Ventilated front seats are excellent and a genuinely impactful feature in Indian heat.
ADAS false alerts in slow traffic are bad enough to make owners disable the system entirely.
Very good 51.4kWh range but the 50kW charging cap is bad for road trips above 300km.
Excellent cabin features overall but rear seat ergonomics are bad for tall passengers.
Service network is very good but real highway range is worse than the advertised ARAI figure.
Build quality is excellent but suspension firmness over potholes is noticeably worse than ICE Creta.
Running cost savings are very good but no wireless Android Auto is a bad daily compromise.

Nine months in Mumbai and the Creta Electric is a very good city EV. Range is excellent for a 70km daily commute. The Hyundai service experience has been very good with two scheduled services completed efficiently. BlueLink app works very well. Overall the best electric SUV purchase decision I have made.

The 50kW DC charging experience is very bad. I drove Hyderabad to Vijayawada and the two charging stops added 100 minutes to my journey time. The charging infrastructure itself is improving but the 50kW car-side limitation makes even good chargers feel bad. This is the worst aspect of Creta Electric ownership.

Cabin quality in the Creta Electric Excellence is very good. Material quality is better than domestic rivals. Dual zone climate control is excellent and cools the cabin quickly even in 42 degree Chennai summers. The overall interior experience is very good. Proud of the purchase decision.

Rear seat comfort is genuinely very bad. On a family trip to Coimbatore all three rear passengers complained throughout the four-hour journey. The knees-up position is a bad ergonomic flaw and the under-thigh support is worse than even budget sedans in this regard. Hyundai must address this in the next model.

ADAS Level 2 on the expressway is excellent. Adaptive cruise control with stop-and-go handled the Bengaluru to Mysore expressway very well. The blind spot monitor is very good and the camera projection on the cluster is an excellent safety feature. The car genuinely feels very safe on highways.

No wireless Android Auto is very bad product management from Hyundai. The technology exists and the car even has a wireless charging pad. Forcing daily cable plugging on a premium product is a bad decision that makes the feature list feel incomplete. This is worse than what some cheaper cars offer today.

The Creta Electric is a very good all-round family EV. Boot space at 433 litres is excellent for family use. Frunk adds very good practical storage. Hyundai reliability gives excellent confidence. The 8-year battery warranty is very good value. Overall an excellent choice for Indian families.

Suspension tuning is bad on Indian roads. The car rides worse than the petrol Creta on our typical road surfaces. Bad pothole impacts are transmitted harshly into the cabin in a way that is worse than expected for a family car. Ride comfort on rough patches is genuinely bad and disappoints daily.

Running cost reduction after switching to Creta Electric is very good. Monthly fuel savings of nearly Rs 6000 versus my old petrol SUV are excellent. Home charging is very convenient and the BlueLink charge scheduling is a very good feature. Total cost of ownership over three years looks excellent.

Real highway range claim versus actual performance is very bad. ARAI says 473km but at 100kmph with AC the 51.4kWh battery gives around 330km. The 42kWh variant is worse with only 280km in the same conditions. This bad gap between claim and reality is misleading buyers in showrooms.
Very good range for city use.
DC charging speed is very bad for highway trips.
Cabin feels premium and spacious.
Rear seat is bad for tall adult passengers.
ADAS on highways works very well.
No wireless projection despite wireless charging is a bad contradiction.
Service and warranty support is very good.
Suspension over potholes is worse than expected.
Ventilated seats are excellent in Indian summers.
Overall decent EV for city-focused buyers.

Took the Creta Electric Excellence on a Lucknow to Agra trip covering 320km. Required one DC charging stop of 55 minutes. The bad wait time aside the car was very good on the highway. ADAS adaptive cruise is excellent and the cabin stayed very comfortable throughout. Very good highway performer when charging is available.

The 50kW DC charging is very bad and makes this a city-only practical car despite the good range. For any trip beyond 350km the charging wait times become a very bad experience. Hyundai needs to urgently upgrade to at least 100kW charging. This is worse than what competitors offer and holding the car back significantly.

The Creta Electric has been a very good purchase for our Kolkata family. Range is excellent for daily use and the home charging routine is very convenient. Hyundai BlueLink app works very well. The ventilated seats are excellent in summer. Overall very happy with the decision to go electric with Hyundai.

Rear seat quality is very bad for a premium SUV. Three adults in the rear is a terrible experience because of the bad floor height and poor thigh support. Even two adults on a journey beyond 60 minutes find the rear experience uncomfortable. This is a bad compromise that significantly affects family usability.

The Creta Electric Excellence is excellent value considering the full feature list. Panoramic sunroof, Bose audio, ventilated seats, 433-litre boot, frunk, V2L and ADAS are very good features in a single package. The build quality is excellent. Running cost reduction is very good. A very satisfying ownership experience overall.

Suspension calibration on bad roads is a very poor aspect of the Creta Electric. The stiffness means every bad pothole registers painfully in the cabin. Compared to the ICE Creta which was excellent for ride comfort the electric version is noticeably worse. This is a bad trade-off that Hyundai made to accommodate battery weight.

BlueLink connected features are very good overall. Remote pre-cooling works excellently in Jaipur summers. Charge scheduling at off-peak tariff is a very good practical feature. Battery health monitoring in the app is very useful. The connected car experience with Hyundai is very good compared to domestic EV rivals.

ADAS false alerts in city are a very bad problem. The forward collision system brakes unnecessarily in slow traffic creating a bad and startling experience for passengers. Several times it triggered with no obstacle present which is dangerously bad calibration. I have disabled ADAS in city mode because the bad alerts outweigh the benefit.

After comparing Creta Electric to MG ZS EV and Tata Nexon EV Max I chose Creta for the excellent service network and very good range. Eleven months in the decision has been validated. Service is excellent and range is consistently very good. The 50kW charging cap is the one bad aspect that rivals do better.

The Creta Electric looks nearly identical to the regular Creta from outside. For a car priced 8-10 lakh above the petrol version the exterior differentiation is very poor. Buyers paying premium for an EV deserve better visual identity. The design similarity to the standard Creta is bad value proposition on aesthetic grounds.
Range is very good but 50kW charging speed is bad for trips.
Cabin features are excellent but rear seat ergonomics are bad.
Service experience is very good but ADAS false alerts are a bad issue.
Build quality is very good but suspension on rough patches is worse than ICE Creta.
Running costs are very good but no wireless Android Auto is a bad compromise.

The Creta Electric is a very good family EV for Indian city conditions. The 51.4kWh battery delivers excellent city range that removes all range anxiety. Ventilated seats, panoramic sunroof and Bose audio make every drive very good. Hyundai's 8-year battery warranty adds excellent ownership confidence. The charging speed is bad but manageable with home charging.

Fifty kilowatt DC charging on the Creta Electric is genuinely very bad in 2024. I drove from Delhi to Chandigarh which is only 270km and still needed a 50-minute charging stop on the way back. Competitors charging at 100kW or above complete the same top-up in half the time. This is a very bad disadvantage that Hyundai needs to urgently address.

Drove the Creta Electric from Pune to Mumbai on the expressway and the ADAS performance was very good. Adaptive cruise handled the 150km stretch excellently. Lane keep assist was smooth. The safety features are excellent and made the drive genuinely less tiring. Very pleased with the ADAS capability on the Expressway.

Rear seat experience is very bad on the Creta Electric. Our family of four regularly uses the car but rear passengers find the knees-up seating bad. The thigh support is poor and the floor is raised because of battery placement. This is a very bad design flaw that makes long family trips uncomfortable for everyone in the back.

The Hyundai Creta Electric Excellence is an excellent city EV and a very good highway car. Build quality is excellent with solid panel fit and good material quality inside. Hyundai service network across India is very good. Running costs at roughly Rs 1.5 per km versus Rs 9 per km for petrol are very good savings. Recommend for urban families.

Wired Android Auto is a very bad daily frustration. Every morning I have to plug in the USB cable which is a bad inconvenience that no premium car should impose. The car has wireless charging on the centre console but not wireless projection. This bad decision makes the feature experience feel worse than cars half the price.

The 51.4kWh battery is the best decision Hyundai made with the Creta Electric. City range of 400-plus km is excellent and removes daily range anxiety completely. Even with AC running continuously city range stays very good above 360km. The range performance alone makes the Creta Electric an excellent choice for city buyers.

Suspension on bad roads is very bad and was my biggest surprise after purchase. I expected the Creta Electric to ride like the ICE Creta which was excellent for comfort. Instead it rides worse over potholes and bad surfaces. Sharp bumps come through harshly. This is a bad compromise and the worst aspect of the car for Indian roads.

Twelve months in Ahmedabad and the Creta Electric has been very good overall. Battery performance is excellent with consistent city range. BlueLink is very good and works reliably. Service centre quality is very good. Running costs have been very good. The only bad aspects are charging speed and the slightly stiffer ride.

ADAS on the Creta Electric is excellent on highways but very bad in city conditions. False collision alerts in bumper-to-bumper traffic happen too frequently. The system is well calibrated for expressways but the bad false positives in city traffic make it unreliable in daily urban use. A very good feature on highways becomes a bad experience in cities.
Excellent real-world range above 400km on 51.4kWh.
50kW DC charging is very bad for any highway travel plan.
Bose audio and panoramic sunroof are excellent cabin highlights.
Rear seat knees-up posture is bad and uncomfortable for adults.
BlueLink remote functions are very good and reliable.
No wireless Android Auto is a bad and inexplicable omission in 2024.
Hyundai warranty and service support is very good.
Suspension over rough city roads is worse than the petrol Creta.
Ventilated seats are excellent and essential in Indian climate.
Overall a decent EV for city-primary buyers.

Fifteen months of Creta Electric ownership in Bengaluru has been excellent. Battery health remains very good per the BlueLink diagnostic report. Running cost savings of Rs 1.5 lakh versus petrol are excellent and real. ADAS has genuinely improved my driving safety. The service experience at my Hyundai centre has been consistently very good. Best car I have owned.

The V2L port placement under the rear seat is very bad design. Using V2L outdoors for camping means you have to open the rear door and reach under the seat which is a bad and inconvenient user experience. The feature itself is good but the bad placement makes it nearly impractical in real use. Hyundai needs to relocate this in the facelift.

The Creta Electric Excellence is a very good package for family buyers. The 433-litre boot is excellent and bigger than most EVs in the segment. Frunk adds very good extra storage for cables. Dual zone AC is excellent for front and rear passenger comfort. Safety features are very good. Overall a very satisfying purchase.

Highway range on the 42kWh variant with AC on is very bad. Only 270 to 290km in real conditions versus the 390km ARAI claim. This is a bad and misleading gap that makes buyers regret choosing the smaller battery. The 42kWh variant is suitable only for city use and advertising it for highway use is bad practice.

The Creta Electric Performance in Eco mode is excellent. City efficiency consistently above 5.5km per kWh is very good. The four regen braking levels are very well calibrated and the highest setting enables excellent one-pedal driving in traffic. Home charging at 7.2kW is very convenient. Very good EV for disciplined city drivers.

ADAS calibration in city is bad. I have experienced two sudden unnecessary braking events in clear road conditions which were scary. The forward collision system's bad sensitivity makes passengers uncomfortable. In one instance the bad false braking nearly caused the vehicle behind me to rear-end me. This level of bad ADAS reliability is unacceptable and dangerous.

The Creta Electric is an excellent choice for buyers coming from the ICE Creta. The familiar design and cabin layout make the transition very comfortable. Feature additions like ventilated seats, larger sunroof and ADAS make the electric version very good overall. Running cost improvement is excellent. Very happy to recommend to other Creta owners.

Exterior design of the Creta Electric is a bad differentiation from the standard Creta. For a car priced nearly 10 lakh higher the design looks almost identical. EV buyers expect a more distinctive visual identity. The design similarity to the petrol version is a bad missed opportunity. Interior is better but exterior identity is a very bad weakness.

The Creta Electric is a very good city commuter for Hyderabad. Range is excellent and the home charging routine established in week one is very convenient. The BlueLink app works very well for tracking charge levels remotely. Service centre visits have been very good experiences. Overall a very good first EV purchase decision.

Real-world range accuracy is a bad problem. The car's estimated range display consistently shows numbers 40 to 60km higher than actual before the battery percentage drops to single digits. This bad range estimation creates false confidence and has left me in bad situations near charger locations twice. Range accuracy is worse than competitors.
Range is excellent for city use.
Charging speed is bad for highway travel.
Build quality feels very good and solid.
Rear seat comfort is bad for long journeys.
Ventilated seats are excellent and very appreciated.
Wireless Android Auto missing is bad product planning.
Service network reliability is very good nationwide.
ADAS false alerts in city are bad and startling.
BlueLink pre-cooling in summers is excellent and very useful.
Overall reasonable city EV with some clear limitations.

Eighteen months of Creta Electric ownership in Pune and I rate the experience very good overall. The 51.4kWh battery range has been excellent and consistently above 400km city. Running cost savings are very good at nearly Rs 7000 per month versus petrol. Hyundai service has been excellent with prompt responses. Only bad aspects are the charging speed and slightly stiffer ride.

The 50kW DC charging is the worst thing about the Creta Electric. I took it from Ahmedabad to Udaipur and the charging stops were a very bad experience. Sixty minutes per stop when nearby cars with 100kW charging finished in 25 minutes was embarrassing. The charging limitation makes the Creta Electric worse than rivals for any meaningful highway use.

The Creta Electric Excellence has excellent overall cabin quality. The pixel DRL design at front gives it a very good distinctive look. Dual screens are very crisp and responsive. The powered driver seat with memory function is a very good premium feature. Bose audio system is excellent. Build quality all round is very good. Proud to own it.

Rear passenger experience in the Creta Electric is very bad. Three members of my family travelling in the rear on our Bengaluru to Coorg trip complained throughout. The bad knees-up posture, poor thigh support and limited recline make the rear experience worse than economy airlines. For a family SUV at this price this is very bad ergonomics.

The Level 2 ADAS in the Creta Electric is excellent when it works correctly on highways. Adaptive cruise with stop-and-go is very good and the lane keep assist is smooth. The blind spot camera on the cluster is an excellent safety innovation. Highway long-distance driving with ADAS is very good. Very pleased with the highway safety features.

No wireless Android Auto on the Creta Electric is a bad omission that Hyundai cannot justify. Every rival at lower prices offers wireless projection. The car has OTA capability so there is no hardware excuse. The bad decision to keep only wired Android Auto makes the infotainment experience worse than it should be at this price.

The Creta Electric is very good value for money when you factor in running costs over five years. Electricity per km is very good at around Rs 1.5 versus Rs 9 for petrol. Maintenance costs are excellent as EV service requirements are minimal. The total cost of ownership picture for the Creta Electric is very good compared to any petrol rival.

Suspension calibration for Indian road conditions is a bad aspect of the Creta Electric. Speed breakers send a jarring impact through the cabin that is worse than the petrol Creta. On bad stretches of road the stiff suspension amplifies every bump harshly. This is very bad ride quality for a car that is supposed to be a comfortable family SUV.

After a year with the Creta Electric Smart 42kWh in Lucknow the experience has been very good for city use. Range is adequate for my 55km daily commute. Home charging is very convenient. Hyundai service is very good and the staff are genuinely knowledgeable about EV specific requirements. A very good first EV purchase.

The exterior identity of the Creta Electric versus the ICE Creta is a very bad weakness. Paying premium for an EV and having neighbours unable to distinguish it from the petrol version is a bad ownership experience for buyers who want recognition of their EV choice. The design differentiation is worse than what BYD and MG offer in the same price range.
City range on 51.4kWh is excellent and class-leading.
50kW DC charging is bad and the worst aspect for highway use.
Hyundai service support is very good across India.
Rear seat posture is bad for adult passengers on longer trips.
433-litre boot and frunk combination is very good for families.
Wired Android Auto cable every day is a bad and avoidable inconvenience.
ADAS on expressways is excellent and genuinely reduces driving fatigue.
Suspension on bad roads is noticeably worse than petrol Creta.
Running cost savings over petrol are very good and real.
Balanced EV for city-focused Indian family buyers overall.

Two years with the Creta Electric Excellence in Delhi NCR and I rate my experience excellent. Battery health at 24 months is very good with minimal degradation. Total running cost savings exceed Rs 2 lakh versus petrol. Hyundai service has been excellent throughout. OTA updates have improved range accuracy and ADAS calibration. The 50kW charging remains the only bad aspect.

The 50kW DC charging cap is very bad and has caused a genuinely bad experience on multiple highway trips. Delhi to Jaipur required a 55-minute charging stop that tested my patience. A rival car with 100kW charging would have taken 25 minutes for the same amount of energy. The Creta Electric charging speed is worse than competition and a bad ownership pain point.

The Creta Electric cabin is excellent in its feature delivery. Panoramic sunroof size is very good and floods the cabin with natural light. Ventilated seats cool down in under 60 seconds which is excellent in Ahmedabad summers. The Bose audio is very good for daily listening. Ambient lighting adds excellent ambience. The cabin experience is genuinely very good.

Rear seat ergonomics on the Creta Electric are a bad design compromise. The battery floor raises the seating position but reduces under-thigh support making long journeys bad for rear passengers. My parents who regularly sit in the rear say the experience is worse than our previous compact sedan. This bad issue significantly reduces family trip usability.

The Creta Electric handles Mumbai to Pune expressway very well. Highway stability at 100kmph is excellent. ADAS adaptive cruise with stop-and-go is very good and reduces fatigue. Range at highway speeds with AC on is very good around 340km on the 51.4kWh. The expressway experience with ADAS is excellent. Very confident on long highway drives.

Exterior design is a bad differentiator from the regular Creta. Standing in a parking lot next to a petrol Creta the differences are minimal for a car priced nearly 10 lakh higher. The bad visual identity is a missed opportunity. EV buyers want their green choice to be visible and the Creta Electric's design is worse than rivals in standing out as an EV.

The Creta Electric is an excellent choice for Bengaluru office commuters. Range is very good for the 60km daily Whitefield to Koramangala commute. Silent operation in traffic is excellent and genuinely reduces commute stress. Running costs are very good. The Hyundai service centre near Electronic City is very good. Highly recommend for IT professional daily commuters.

Infotainment system occasionally lags during navigation rerouting which is a bad experience during active driving. The system freezes for 10 to 15 seconds which is a bad and distracting response time. For a car with premium pricing this bad software performance is below the standard expected. Rivals like the BYD Atto have better infotainment responsiveness.

The Creta Electric is a very good overall city EV for Indian families. Build quality is excellent. Range is very good. Features are impressive. Service support is very good. The bad aspects of charging speed, rear seat ergonomics and no wireless Android Auto are real limitations but do not outweigh the very good strengths for city-primary buyers.

Cold morning battery performance is bad. On foggy Chandigarh mornings in winter the range drops to a bad 310km from the usual 420km because of poor battery temperature management. The bad cold weather performance is a real limitation. The car needs active battery preconditioning for cold weather which is absent and makes winter range worse than acceptable.
Very good city range and excellent features.
Charging speed at 50kW is bad and urgently needs improvement.
Hyundai build quality and service are very good.
Rear seat is bad and falls short of family SUV expectations.
Ventilated seats and Bose audio are excellent highlights.
Wired Android Auto only is a bad daily inconvenience.
ADAS on highway is very good and genuinely useful.
Ride quality over bad roads is worse than the petrol version.
Running costs and savings from electric are very good.
Overall decent family EV with clear strengths and limitations.

After nine months in Chennai the Creta Electric has delivered excellent city performance. The 51.4kWh battery is very good in hot weather conditions with AC running. Remote pre-cooling via BlueLink before entering the car is excellent and genuinely essential in Chennai summers. Service centre quality is very good. The car is excellent for Tamil Nadu city conditions overall.

The Creta Electric is bad value for highway-focused buyers. The 50kW DC charging makes every charging stop a bad waiting experience. The highway range of around 320km at 100kmph on the large battery requires too many stops for drives above 500km. For buyers who need highway capability the Creta Electric is worse than petrol alternatives on practical terms.

The Creta Electric offers very good running economics. Monthly electricity cost for 1500km of city driving is around Rs 900 compared to Rs 10500 for petrol. The savings are excellent and accumulate very quickly. Combined with very good low maintenance requirements the Creta Electric is an excellent long-term ownership decision for regular city commuters.

Wired Android Auto is a very bad decision that Hyundai refuses to fix via OTA. The hardware for wireless projection exists but the feature is disabled. This is a very bad practice of holding back features that cost nothing to enable. The bad daily cable routine makes the infotainment experience worse than competitors who enabled wireless projection as standard.

The Creta Electric Smart variant at around 18 lakh is a very good value proposition. Range on 42kWh is very good for city use up to 100km daily. Cabin features including ADAS, panoramic sunroof and dual screens are excellent. Service network is very good. The bad aspects of charging speed and rear ergonomics are the same but the value is better at this variant level.

The Creta Electric exterior design disappointment is very bad for resale perception. When prospective used car buyers cannot immediately identify it as an EV versus regular Creta the bad visual differentiation affects perceived premium value. The near-identical design is a bad strategy that may hurt second-owner market confidence in the years ahead.

The Creta Electric is an excellent choice for first-time EV buyers coming from Hyundai ICE models. The familiar brand experience makes the transition very comfortable. Features like BlueLink are very good. Range is excellent for daily needs. Hyundai service reliability is very good. The car makes EV adoption feel very good rather than risky.

ADAS city performance is genuinely bad and a safety concern. Forward collision system has triggered unnecessarily at low speeds multiple times. Once it braked hard at 30kmph with no obstacle causing the car behind to swerve badly. This bad unreliable ADAS behaviour in city conditions is worse than helpful and creates more danger than it prevents.

The Creta Electric Excellence is an excellent product for city-focused Indian family needs. Very good range, excellent features, good build quality, very good service support and excellent running economics make it the best family EV available in India. The bad aspects are charging speed and rear seat comfort which need fixing in the next update.

Battery thermal management in winter is bad. On cold Dehradun mornings the range drops to very bad levels around 290km from the usual 420km city range. The lack of battery preconditioning before charging makes DC charging in cold weather slower and worse than in warm conditions. Cold weather EV management is a bad weakness of the Creta Electric.
51.4kWh real-world city range is the best in segment.
DC fast charging at 50kW is bad and outdated in 2024.
Cabin quality and features are excellent and class-leading.
Rear seat ergonomics are bad and hurt family trip comfort.
Hyundai service network is very good and widely accessible.
ADAS false alerts in city are bad and cause startling moments.
Bose audio and ventilated seats are excellent feature highlights.
Suspension on bad road patches is worse than the petrol Creta.
Running costs versus petrol savings are very good and real.
Overall a good EV for city-focused buyers with home charging.

The Creta Electric Excellence is an excellent buy for Kolkata city commuters. Range on 51.4kWh is very good and consistent above 400km city. Hyundai service in Kolkata is very good. Build quality is excellent. Features like ventilated seats, panoramic sunroof and ADAS are very good additions. Running cost savings are excellent. Only bad issue is the 50kW charging on highway trips.

Owning the Creta Electric for highway-heavy use has been a bad experience. I drive 250km every weekend between two cities and the 50kW DC charging adds a very bad 55-minute stop each way. The bad wait time every weekend for five months has been frustrating. For high-mileage highway users the Creta Electric is a worse option than petrol until charging speed improves.

The panoramic sunroof on the Creta Electric is excellent. Size is very good and the cabin feels bright and airy. The UV-cut glass keeps heat manageable. Tilt and slide operation is smooth and the sunshade quality is very good. The sunroof enhances the overall premium feel of the car significantly. A very good feature that adds excellent value to daily driving.

Rear seat design is a very bad compromise on the Creta Electric. Three adults in the rear is a bad experience because of the raised floor forcing a worse seating angle. Even two adults find the thigh support bad on journeys beyond 90 minutes. This bad seating ergonomics is the worst aspect of the Creta Electric for genuine family use.

The Creta Electric is a very good city car with excellent running economics. Buying it for a 60km daily commute with home charging is an excellent decision. Range anxiety is completely eliminated. BlueLink features are very good. Service is reliable. Running costs are excellent. The Creta Electric is very good for exactly this type of usage pattern.

Infotainment software is bad on occasions. Navigation rerouting causes the screen to freeze for 15 seconds which is a bad and distracting experience. The Bluetooth connection drops occasionally which is a bad reliability issue. For a premium car this bad software behaviour is below expectations. Tata and MG have better infotainment software stability than the Creta Electric.

The Creta Electric is excellent value over five years of ownership. Running cost savings are very good. Low maintenance requirements are excellent. The 8-year battery warranty gives very good protection. Resale value should be very good given the Creta brand strength. The total cost of ownership makes the Creta Electric an excellent financial decision versus petrol.

Cold weather range reduction is very bad. In December in Shimla the range dropped to 280km from the usual 420km. This bad 33 percent winter range reduction is worse than acceptable. The absence of battery preconditioning before driving in cold conditions makes the bad range drop worse. Cold climate buyers should seriously consider this bad limitation before purchasing.

The Creta Electric Excellence is an excellent family EV and the best in its price range in India. Range is very good. Features are excellent. Safety is very good. Service is excellent. Running costs are very good. It is not perfect with the bad charging speed and rear seat posture but overall it is a very good decision for Indian family buyers.

The Creta Electric design versus ICE Creta is a bad differentiator. Neighbours, colleagues and family members cannot tell the difference between the electric and petrol Creta in a parking lot. For buyers who want their green commitment to be visually recognised the bad visual identity of the Creta Electric is a worse proposition than the MG ZS EV or BYD Atto 3.
Excellent range and very good features overall.
50kW charging is bad and the biggest weakness of this car.
Service network quality and coverage is very good nationwide.
Rear seat discomfort is bad and a genuine family trip issue.
BlueLink remote pre-cooling feature is excellent in Indian summers.
No wireless Android Auto is a bad and daily frustrating omission.
ADAS on highway is excellent and reduces long-drive fatigue.
Suspension is worse than ICE Creta on bad roads and potholes.
Ventilated seats and Bose audio are genuinely excellent features.
Acceptable city EV with good but not perfect execution.

Twenty months of Creta Electric ownership in Hyderabad and the overall experience has been very good. Battery performance has been excellent with no detectable degradation. Running cost savings are very good at nearly Rs 8000 per month. Hyundai service quality has been very good and responsive. BlueLink OTA updates have improved the car meaningfully. Very good long-term EV ownership.

The 50kW DC charging limitation ruined a family trip from Mumbai to Nashik. A 170km drive required a 50-minute charging stop because of bad range management with AC and full passenger load. The bad charging speed added stress to what should have been a relaxing trip. The 50kW cap is the worst design decision on the Creta Electric and a very bad owner pain point.

The Creta Electric is excellent for Gurugram city commuters. Range on 51.4kWh is very good and covers weekly needs with just one home charging session. The Level 2 ADAS on the Delhi-Gurugram expressway is excellent and reduces morning commute fatigue. Hyundai service centre in Gurugram is very good. Highly recommend for NCR corporate commuters.

The rear seat on the Creta Electric is bad for our family. My parents in their 60s find the seating posture very bad for their back and knees. The elevated floor creates a bad angle that puts strain on older passengers. After one bad long journey they refused to travel in the rear again. This is a very bad design failure for a car positioned as a family SUV.

The Creta Electric Excellence is a very good package overall. The combination of excellent range, very good features and Hyundai's brand reliability makes it the best family EV available in India today. Yes the charging speed is bad and the rear seat is worse than expected but for the 90 percent of city use the car is excellent. Very satisfied after 14 months of ownership.

Winter driving range on the Creta Electric is bad. My Chandigarh based Creta Electric drops to around 290km in December versus the excellent 420km in summer. The bad cold weather range reduction means winter driving requires more frequent charging which is a bad inconvenience. Cold weather battery management on the Creta Electric is worse than what premium European EVs offer.

The frunk is an excellent addition to the Creta Electric. The 22-litre frunk storage for charging cables is very good and keeps the 433-litre boot completely free. The frunk opens smoothly and closes with a very good quality feel. Practical storage planning on the Creta Electric is excellent. Very good EV-specific engineering that adds real daily value.

ADAS in city is genuinely bad. Forward collision system braking unnecessarily at junctions, false lane departure warnings and bad sensitivity calibration make the ADAS experience worse than helpful in urban conditions. Hyundai has released OTA updates but the city ADAS behaviour remains bad. Until calibration improves significantly the ADAS is better disabled in city mode.

The Creta Electric offers excellent value for city-primary buyers over three to five years of ownership. Running costs are very good. Maintenance is excellent in its simplicity. The 8-year battery warranty gives very good protection against the biggest EV risk. Hyundai's service network reliability is very good. The total ownership proposition is excellent for urban commuters.

The Creta Electric is bad value for buyers who need highway capable fast charging. At this price you expect at least 100kW charging. The 50kW cap makes every highway stop a bad 60-minute wait. Competitors at lower prices offer better charging speeds. The bad charging experience on highways makes the car worse than petrol alternatives for buyers who regularly drive more than 200km in a day.
Very good 400km-plus city range is the standout positive.
50kW charging cap is bad and the most criticised aspect consistently.
Cabin feature richness is excellent and class-leading in segment.
Rear seat ergonomics are bad and need redesign in next generation.
Hyundai brand reliability and service quality are very good.
Wired Android Auto only is a bad and unjustifiable omission.
ADAS on highway is very good and makes long drives genuinely safer.
Suspension is worse than the petrol Creta on rough roads.
Running cost savings are very good and accumulate significantly.
Good overall EV with clear strengths and some genuine weaknesses.

After evaluating every EV SUV under 25 lakh I chose the Creta Electric for its excellent range and very good service network. Sixteen months later the decision remains very good. Battery performance is excellent. BlueLink features are very good and reliable. Running costs are excellent. ADAS has been very good on expressways. The bad 50kW charging is the only persistent regret.

My experience with the Creta Electric as a highway driver has been very bad. I drive Pune to Nashik weekly which is 220km. The 50kW charging at Nashik takes a bad 50 minutes before heading back. Every week this bad wait repeats. For weekly highway commuters the Creta Electric charging speed makes it worse than a reliable petrol SUV in practice.

The Creta Electric has been excellent for our Vizag family. City range is very good. The 433-litre boot handles beach trip luggage very well. Ventilated seats in coastal Andhra heat are excellent. Hyundai service in Vizag is very good. Running costs are excellent versus petrol. Overall a very good and reliable family EV for our South Indian coastal city use.

Rear seat quality is very bad and the most consistently disappointing aspect of the Creta Electric. Every family trip review from rear passengers is bad regarding comfort. The bad floor height and poor recline create worse conditions than our previous compact hatchback for rear passengers. Hyundai must prioritise rear ergonomics in the next generation as this bad issue hurts real-world family usability significantly.

The Creta Electric is excellent for Bengaluru's IT corridor daily commuting. Range on 51.4kWh is very good and handles the 80km round trip from Whitefield to Indiranagar effortlessly. Silent operation in traffic is excellent. Home charging overnight at the apartment is very convenient. Hyundai service centre on Outer Ring Road is very good. An excellent city EV recommendation for tech professionals.
City range on 51.4kWh battery is excellent and best in segment.
50kW DC charging speed is bad and needs urgent improvement from Hyundai.
Panoramic sunroof and ventilated seats are excellent practical features for India.
Rear seat knees-up posture is a bad and consistent ergonomic complaint.
BlueLink remote pre-cooling and charge scheduling are very good features.
No wireless Android Auto at this price is a bad and frustrating omission.
ADAS Level 2 on highways is very good and genuinely reduces fatigue.
Suspension over potholes is worse than ICE Creta and transmits bad impacts.
Hyundai 8-year battery warranty and service network are very good advantages.
Overall good but not perfect EV with clear priorities needed in next gen.

The Hyundai Creta Electric is the best family EV available in India under 25 lakh. Excellent range, very good features, very good service and excellent safety. The bad aspects like 50kW charging, rear seat posture and no wireless Android Auto are real but do not undermine the very good overall ownership proposition for city-primary Indian family buyers.

DC charging at 50kW is a very bad bottleneck on the Creta Electric. I have tested multiple public 150kW chargers which throttle down to 50kW immediately when the Creta connects. Watching a Tata Curvv EV charge at 100kW next to me while my Creta maxes at 50kW is a very bad and frustrating visible reminder of the Hyundai's worst limitation.

The Creta Electric is an excellent option for buyers upgrading from an ICE Creta. Familiar cabin logic, better features and electric efficiency. The transition was very comfortable and the overall experience is very good. Running costs are excellent. Range is very good. Hyundai service is excellent. Would recommend the upgrade to any existing Creta owner.

V2L port placement under the rear seat is genuinely bad. To power a camping device you have to open the rear door and reach awkwardly under the seat which is a bad and inconvenient user experience. The V2L feature itself is very good but the bad placement ruins practical use. Hyundai should move the V2L port to a more accessible exterior location in the facelift.

The Creta Electric is a very good choice for Kochi's urban driving. City range is excellent for the 50km daily commute. Hyundai service quality in Kochi is very good. Running cost savings versus petrol are excellent. The ventilated seats are a very good feature in Kerala's humid climate. Overall a very good city EV for South Indian coastal urban buyers.

Infotainment lag is a bad software problem that persists despite OTA updates. Screen freezes for 10 to 15 seconds during navigation rerouting. Bluetooth drops once or twice a week which is a bad reliability failure. For a premium 20-plus lakh car this bad software quality is worse than what you get in mainstream segment cars that cost much less.

The Creta Electric Excellence is excellent overall. The combination of very good range, excellent features, Hyundai's very good service network and excellent safety credentials makes it the most well-rounded EV for Indian families. The bad 50kW charging and bad rear ergonomics need fixing but the car is very good in its core city-family EV mission.

Real highway range on 42kWh variant is very bad. Only 270 to 280km at 100kmph with AC. The ARAI 390km claim is a very bad misrepresentation of real highway use. Buying the 42kWh variant expecting highway capability is a bad decision that the marketing does not adequately warn buyers about. The 42kWh is fine for city but bad for any highway use above 200km.

The Creta Electric is a very good smart purchase for Chandigarh city driving. Range is excellent for the planned city grid. Silent operation is very good. Hyundai service network is excellent in the city. Running costs are very good. BlueLink works well. The car is a very good fit for Chandigarh's clean, wide roads and excellent infrastructure for EV ownership.

ADAS false positive braking alerts in city traffic are very bad. I have had three incidents where the forward collision system triggered without any real obstacle causing my passengers to jolt forward uncomfortably. This bad unreliable behaviour in urban conditions has made me disable ADAS in city mode. A bad safety feature that creates unsafe moments is worse than having no ADAS at all.
Very good city range makes daily use completely worry-free.
50kW DC charging is bad and makes highway trips stressful.
Hyundai service and warranty coverage are very good and reassuring.
Rear seat ergonomics are bad and the biggest family trip limitation.
Ventilated seats are excellent and essential for Indian summers.
Wired Android Auto only is bad and daily frustrating without good reason.
433-litre boot and frunk combination is very good for family storage.
Suspension on rough patches is noticeably worse than the ICE Creta.
Running cost savings over petrol are very good and accumulate well.
Decent family EV for city use with room for improvement on key aspects.

The Creta Electric has been excellent over two years of daily use in Delhi. Battery health remains very good. Running cost savings are excellent and total nearly Rs 2.5 lakh. Hyundai service has been very good throughout. The car performs very good in city conditions every single day. The charging speed remains bad but home charging covers 95 percent of needs. Overall excellent EV ownership.

Bought the Creta Electric expecting good highway range and was badly disappointed. Real highway range of 310km at 100kmph is bad when the marketing claims 473km. Two highway trips from Bengaluru to Mysore and back showed the bad claim-versus-reality gap every time. The ARAI range figure is a very bad misrepresentation of real highway driving conditions.

The Creta Electric cabin is excellent. Dual screens, Bose audio, ventilated seats, panoramic sunroof and ambient lighting create a very good premium feel. Material quality is better than domestic rivals. The overall interior experience is very good and justifies the premium price over ICE Creta. Best cabin in the segment for family buyers without doubt.

Rear seat comfort is genuinely the worst aspect of the Creta Electric for families. Three family members in the rear described their experience during a four-hour drive as bad throughout. The bad knees-up angle, poor thigh support and inadequate recline made the drive worse than expected. This bad rear seat experience has made us take a petrol car for any trip above two hours.

The Creta Electric is a very good smart purchase for Jaipur city use. Range is excellent for the city driving pattern. Hyundai service in Jaipur has been very good. BlueLink remote cooling is excellent in Rajasthan summers. Running costs are very good. Battery performed well through the hot Jaipur summer. Very happy with the Creta Electric as a daily city car overall.

50kW charging speed is very bad and the Creta Electric's single biggest design failure. At 24 lakh for the Excellence variant the 50kW cap is indefensible. Cars at 15 lakh offer 100kW charging. Hyundai charging at 50kW in 2024 makes every public charging stop a bad experience. This bad limitation keeps the Creta Electric worse than its potential for long-distance users.

The Creta Electric is a very good family EV for Indian conditions. Build quality is excellent. Range is very good. Safety with ADAS is excellent. Service support is very good. Running economics are excellent over time. The bad rear seat, bad charging speed and bad absence of wireless Android Auto are real flaws but the car remains very good for city-family use overall.

ADAS calibration in city is a very bad problem. The forward collision warning and auto-braking trigger unnecessarily at slow city speeds multiple times per week. This bad over-sensitivity is worse than helpful and has startled passengers badly on several occasions. One of my passengers now refuses to ride with ADAS on because the bad false alerts are too frightening for them.

The Creta Electric is excellent for Surat city commuters. Range is very good for typical 65km daily commute. Home charging is very convenient. Hyundai service quality in Surat is very good. BlueLink features are excellent. Running costs are very good at approximately Rs 900 per month for 1500km. Overall an excellent and well-rounded city EV purchase decision.

The exterior of the Creta Electric is a bad missed opportunity for EV identity. Hyundai should have given the Creta Electric a more distinctive design to justify the premium price over the petrol version. The near-identical exterior is a bad visual differentiation that makes the EV version look like a poorly badged variant rather than a thoughtfully designed electric vehicle.
Excellent 51.4kWh range and very good cabin features throughout.
DC 50kW charging is bad and ruins highway trip planning convenience.
Hyundai service reliability is very good across all major Indian cities.
Rear seat experience on long trips is bad for adults consistently.
BlueLink and ventilated seats are very good and excellent for India.
Wired-only Android Auto is bad and below expectations at this price.
Running cost savings are very good and one of the best ownership benefits.
Suspension calibration is worse than ICE Creta and bad over rough surfaces.
ADAS on highway is excellent but bad in city due to frequent false alerts.

The Hyundai Creta Electric is a very good family EV with excellent range, very good features and very good service support. The bad charging speed at 50kW, bad rear seat ergonomics, bad wired-only Android Auto and worse suspension than ICE Creta are real and significant weaknesses. Overall it remains the best family electric SUV in India today despite these bad limitations.
